title: Context Engineering
course: Agentic Development with GitHub Copilot CLI, LakeBank, SyteLine, and MCP
prerequisites: Learner can start a safe GitHub Copilot CLI session.
Learner understands the repo type and safe workflow from Module 3.
summary: Context engineering is the practice of deliberately giving GitHub Copilot CLI the right information, constraints, files, requirements, and workflow boundaries before asking it to help.
A weak prompt asks Copilot to guess.
A strong context-engineered prompt tells Copilot:
- what repo type it is in
- what output artifact should be created
whyItMatters: GitHub Copilot works better when it has enough context. GitHub's documentation describes custom instructions as a way to automatically provide project, team, and tool context so Copilot can generate more tailored responses. GitHub's prompt engineering guidance also emphasizes starting with the goal and then adding specific requirements.
For your team, context quality is especially important because different repos need different workflows:
- LakeBank product repos may already have memory bank, instructions, and skills.
- Older product repos intentionally have no LakeBank, no repo instructions, no skills, no agents, and no memory bank.
- Customer modification repos may or may not have SyteLine plugin support.
- Some work may need MCP evidence from SQL Server MCP, Power-Flex MCP, or VersionOneMCP.
- Some work is safe for read-only research only.
- Some work requires phased plans and human approval before code changes.
Context engineering reduces guessing and makes Copilot's output easier to verify.
howItFits: Modules 2 and 1 established CLI fundamentals and developer accountability.
Module 4 teaches how to turn available evidence into better prompts.
This is the first module in the research-planning-creative sequence.
Later modules will use context engineering to create:
learningObjectives: Explain why context engineering is different from simply writing a long prompt.
Identify useful context sources in a repo.
Choose context differently based on repo classification.
Use file references to ground Copilot in specific artifacts.
Decide whether context belongs in custom instructions, a prompt, a skill, memory bank, or a temporary markdown file.
Write a context-engineered prompt with goal, constraints, evidence, output, and verification rules.
Review Copilot output for missing context and unsupported claims.
keyConceptsMarkdown: ### Concept 1: Context engineering is deliberate context selection
Context engineering is not dumping every file into a prompt.
It is selecting the most useful context for the task.
### Concept 2: Different context sources have different purposes
Not all context belongs in the same place.
| Context source | Best for | Avoid using it for |
| Prompt text | One-time task instructions and constraints | Durable project rules |
| File references | Grounding Copilot in exact files | Vague repo-wide discovery |
| Custom instructions | Ongoing repo/team guidance | One-off task details |
| Prompt files | Reusable task prompts | Always-on behavior |
| Skills | Multi-step workflows with resources | Simple style preferences |
| GitHub Gists | Personal reusable snippets, checklists, mini-playbooks | Shared team guidance (use repo instructions instead) |
| VersionOne story | Requirements and acceptance criteria | Repo architecture |
| SQL query result | Database evidence | General product behavior without review |
Do not add or modify custom instructions in this module.
### Concept 3: Good prompts have a structure
A strong context-engineered prompt usually includes:
Copilot may sound confident even when it is guessing.
For each important claim, cite the file or artifact that supports it. If you cannot verify the claim, label it as an assumption or unknown.
The goal is not just better output. The goal is reviewable output.
Do not ask Copilot to go straight from vague request to implementation.
This module focuses on Stage 2 and Stage 3.
### Concept 6: Context hygiene prevents future mistakes
- do not mix temporary task notes into durable memory
- do not put customer-sensitive data into general course artifacts
- do not add repo instructions casually
- remove or correct wrong AI-generated notes
- do not reuse old context blindly
Bad context is worse than no context because it can mislead future work.
Use this checklist when deciding what to give Copilot.
- [ ] memory-bank files, if present and appropriate
Use this pattern throughout the rest of the course.
After creating the artifact, list what the developer should verify before continuing.
Tell me what context I need.
- no distinction between durable and temporary context
Do not install or modify plugins.
- recommended context files for future research prompts
- what might belong in durable memory later, but should not be updated now
- what should come from a human
- what should come from MCP later
- prompt template for the next research step
Copilot CLI can reference files outside the current repo if the path is accessible on your local file system.
- You are working in a customer modification repo and need to reference a Product repo for standards.
- You are comparing your implementation against a reference implementation in another local repo.
- You want to use a prompt or skill from a plugin repo stored locally.
- You want to reference documentation from another team's local clone.
- Only reference paths you have access to.
- Do not reference paths that contain customer-specific data, secrets, or production connection strings.
- Do not reference paths outside your team's expected scope without approval.
- Cross-repo references do not persist — provide them again in new sessions if needed.
This technique is especially valuable when the target repo lacks its own instructions or memory bank.
### Concept 8: Copilot as a knowledge base for standards
GitHub Copilot CLI can act as a queryable knowledge base when given the right context.
- Asking about your company's coding standards.
- Asking whether a new approach matches team conventions.
- Getting a quick explanation of a skill, agent, or instruction.
- Asking whether a change is consistent with team architecture decisions.
- Learning what a team decision was without hunting through documentation.
What is the team's approved pattern for handling database errors in this codebase? Cite the pattern name and the source file.
What does this repo instruct Copilot to do or avoid? List the key rules.
Based on this guidance, what is the approved error handling pattern for this type of change? Cite specifically.
- Memory bank files are up to date.
- Custom instructions are curated and specific.
- Skills or agents reference specific standards files.
Copilot can give wrong or outdated answers if the standards files are stale or missing.
Always verify that Copilot's answer matches the actual referenced file.

lab.title: Create a context map for future agentic work
beforeYouStart: I opened a terminal in the target repo.
I checked the working tree status.
I cleared stale context or started a fresh session.
I know this lab is read-only.
I will not add repo instructions.
I will not install or modify plugins.
I will not modify MCP configuration.

prompts.label: Lab prompt
promptText: We are practicing context engineering for an internal agentic development course.
- Do not add, remove, or change MCP configuration.
- You may inspect files and folders.
If these files exist, use them as supporting context:
Summarize the repo classification and confidence level from docs/repo-classification.md.
List files, folders, docs, instructions, memory files, plugin references, MCP configuration, build files, test files, and other context sources that exist.
List which files should be referenced with @ for future research or planning prompts and explain why.
List context that appears missing or unclear.
List what should be captured in docs task-specific files.
List questions a developer, product owner, support engineer, QA tester, or senior developer should answer before future work.
List whether SQL Server MCP, Power-Flex MCP, or VersionOneMCP might be useful later and what each might provide. Do not call or configure MCP in this module.
## Prompt Template for Next Research Step
Create a reusable prompt template that includes:
- Do not treat assumptions as facts.
- Keep temporary context separate from durable memory.

prompts.label: Optional improvement prompt
promptText: Review docs/context-engineering-notes.md as a skeptical senior developer.
- places where the next research prompt is too vague
- places where customer, SQL, Power-Flex, VersionOne, LakeBank, or SyteLine context may be needed later
Update only docs/context-engineering-notes.md if improvements are needed.

expectedContents: prompt template for next research step

verificationChecklist: Did Copilot correctly summarize the repo classification?
Did Copilot identify real context files and folders?
Did Copilot avoid inventing context sources?
Did Copilot recommend useful file references for future prompts?
Did Copilot separate temporary working context from durable context?
Did Copilot avoid durable memory updates beyond `docs/context-engineering-notes.md`?
Did Copilot avoid source code changes?
Did Copilot avoid repo instruction changes?
Did Copilot avoid MCP configuration changes?
Did Copilot identify human context needed?
Did Copilot identify possible MCP context for later?
Did Copilot provide a usable next research prompt template?
Did Copilot label assumptions and unknowns?

evidence.summaryPrompt: Write 3-5 sentences describing the most useful context sources Copilot found and what context is still missing.
reflectionQuestions: What context source looked most useful for future prompts?
What context was missing or unclear?
What context should stay temporary in docs?
What context might belong in durable memory later, after verification?
What human input is needed before future research or planning?
What MCP server might be useful later, and why?
Was the next research prompt template specific enough?

skillCheck.question: What is context engineering?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Context engineering is the deliberate selection and organization of relevant information, constraints, files, requirements, and evidence so Copilot can produce better and more reviewable output. It is not simply writing a long prompt or dumping every file into context.

skillCheck.question: What is the difference between temporary working context and durable memory?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Temporary working context is task-specific information stored in files such as `docs/research.md`, `docs/plan.md`, or `docs/context-engineering-notes.md`. Durable memory is verified, long-lived repo knowledge that belongs in memory bank or persistent documentation. Temporary guesses should not be written to durable memory.

skillCheck.question: Why is evidence important in Copilot output?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Evidence makes Copilot output reviewable. A confident answer without file, test, story, or data evidence may be a guess. Important claims should cite a source or be labeled as assumptions or unknowns.

skillCheck.question: When should custom instructions be updated?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Custom instructions should only be updated when repo or team guidance needs durable always-on behavior and the team has approved it. They should not be changed during temporary context collection or one-off task research.

skillCheck.question: When might a skill be better than a prompt?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: A skill is better when the task is a reusable multi-step workflow that needs instructions, scripts, resources, and consistent execution. A one-off prompt is better for a single task that does not need reusable workflow packaging.

summaryMarkdown: In this module, you learned how to turn repo classification into better Copilot prompts.
You should now understand that good context engineering includes:
You also learned that different context sources serve different purposes:
- prompt files for reusable task prompts
- memory bank for durable verified knowledge
- human input for requirements and judgment
The goal is to make Copilot easier to guide and easier to review.
readinessChecklist: I can explain context engineering in my own words.
I can explain why longer prompts are not automatically better prompts.
I can identify context sources in a repo.
I can use repo classification to choose context strategy.
I can write a prompt with goal, context, allowed actions, prohibited actions, evidence rules, and output artifact.
I can separate temporary context from durable memory.
I can identify when human input is needed.
I can identify when MCP may be useful later.
I can review Copilot output for unsupported claims.
I can explain why this module does not update instructions, plugins, MCP configuration, source code, or memory bank.
commonMistakes: Thinking context engineering means adding more words.
Dumping too many unrelated files into context.
Forgetting to specify allowed and prohibited actions.
Updating memory bank with temporary notes.
Adding custom instructions for a one-off task.
Confusing MCP data with repo documentation.
Using stale Module 1 or Module 2 artifacts without review.
review.nextModuleTeaser:
Module 6: Research Before Planning

Next you will convert context into evidence and document what is known before creating a plan.
